Abiogenesis.definition: The original evolution of life or living organisms from inorganic or inanimate substances.

Absolute Zero.definition: The lowest possible temperature, at which all molecular motion ceases (0 Kelvin, or -273.15°C).

Action Potential.definition: A rapid change in voltage across a cellular membrane, typically in neurons, allowing the transmission of signals.

Algorithm.definition: A finite set of unambiguous instructions that, given some set of initial conditions, can be performed in a prescribed sequence to achieve a certain goal.

Allele.definition: One of two or more alternative forms of a gene that arise by mutation and are found at the same place on a chromosome.

Angiosperm.definition: A plant that has flowers and produces seeds enclosed within a carpel.

Antimatter.definition: Matter composed of the antiparticles of those that constitute normal matter, possessing the same mass but opposite charge.

Apoptosis.definition: The death of cells which occurs as a normal and controlled part of an organism's growth or development.

Astrocyte.definition: A star-shaped glial cell of the central nervous system that provides nutrients to neurons and maintains the blood-brain barrier.

Astrophysics.definition: The branch of astronomy concerned with the physical nature of stars and other celestial bodies.

Autophagy.definition: The natural, regulated mechanism of the cell that disassembles unnecessary or dysfunctional components.

Bacteriophage.definition: A virus that infects and replicates within bacteria.

Baryon.definition: A subatomic particle, such as a proton or neutron, made up of three quarks.

Beta Decay.definition: A type of radioactive decay in which a beta particle (fast energetic electron or positron) is emitted.

Bioinformatics.definition: The use of computer technology to manage and analyze large sets of biological data.

Biomimicry.definition: The design and production of materials, structures, and systems that are modeled on biological entities and processes.

Black Hole.definition: A region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, including light, can escape from it.

Blood-Brain Barrier.definition: A highly selective semipermeable border that prevents solutes in the circulating blood from non-selectively crossing into the extracellular fluid of the central nervous system.

Bose-Einstein Condensate.definition: A state of matter of a dilute gas of bosons cooled to temperatures very close to absolute zero.

Brownian Motion.definition: The random, uncontrolled movement of particles in a fluid as they collide with surrounding molecules.

Cambrian Explosion.definition: An event approximately 541 million years ago when most major animal phyla appeared in the fossil record.

Capillary Action.definition: The ability of a liquid to flow in narrow spaces without the assistance of, or even in opposition to, external forces like gravity.

Catalyst.definition: A substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without itself undergoing any permanent chemical change.

Cellular Respiration.definition: The process by which organisms use oxygen to break down food molecules to get chemical energy for cell functions.

Centripetal Force.definition: A force that acts on a body moving in a circular path and is directed toward the center around which the body is moving.

Chaos Theory.definition: A branch of mathematics focusing on behavior that is highly sensitive to initial conditions, popularly known as the butterfly effect.

Chemosynthesis.definition: The synthesis of organic compounds by bacteria or other living organisms using energy derived from reactions involving inorganic chemicals.

Chirality.definition: A property of asymmetry important in several branches of science; an object or a system is chiral if it is distinguishable from its mirror image.

CRISPR.definition: A technology that can be used to edit genes, allowing researchers to alter DNA sequences and modify gene function.

Cryogenics.definition: The branch of physics that deals with the production and effects of very low temperatures.

Cytoplasm.definition: The material or protoplasm within a living cell, excluding the nucleus.

Dark Matter.definition: A form of matter thought to account for approximately 85% of the matter in the universe, which does not emit or interact with electromagnetic radiation.

Dark Energy.definition: A theoretical form of energy that tends to accelerate the expansion of the universe.

Deep Learning.definition: A subset of machine learning based on artificial neural networks with multiple layers (hence 'deep').

Dendrite.definition: A short branched extension of a nerve cell, along which impulses received from other cells are transmitted to the cell body.

DNA Sequencing.definition: The process of determining the nucleic acid sequence—the order of nucleotides in DNA.

Doppler Effect.definition: An increase (or decrease) in the frequency of sound, light, or other waves as the source and observer move toward (or away from) each other.

Dopamine.definition: A neurotransmitter that plays several important roles in the brain and body, including reward-motivated behavior.

Drosophila.definition: A genus of small flies, frequently used in genetics research as a model organism.

Dysbiosis.definition: An imbalance between the types of organism present in a person's natural microflora, especially that of the gut.

Ecliptic.definition: A great circle on the celestial sphere representing the sun's apparent path during the year.

Eigenvalue.definition: Each of a set of values of a parameter for which a differential equation has a nonzero solution under given conditions.

Electromagnetism.definition: One of the four fundamental forces of nature, responsible for the interaction between electrically charged particles.

Endocytosis.definition: The taking in of matter by a living cell by invagination of its membrane to form a vacuole.

Enthalpy.definition: A thermodynamic quantity equivalent to the total heat content of a system.

Entropy.definition: A thermodynamic property that is a measure of the system's thermal energy per unit temperature that is unavailable for doing useful work; often described as a measure of disorder.

Epigenetics.definition: The study of changes in organisms caused by modification of gene expression rather than alteration of the genetic code itself.

Event Horizon.definition: The boundary surrounding a black hole from which no light or matter can escape.

Evolution.definition: The change in the heritable characteristics of biological populations over successive generations.

Exoplanet.definition: A planet that orbits a star outside the solar system.

Fermi Paradox.definition: The conflict between the high probability of extraterrestrial civilizations and the lack of evidence for, or contact with, such civilizations.

Fibonacci Sequence.definition: A series of numbers where each number is the sum of the two preceding ones, often found in natural biological patterns.

Fission.definition: A nuclear reaction in which a heavy nucleus splits spontaneously or on impact with another particle, releasing energy.

Fluorescence.definition: The visible or invisible radiation emitted by certain substances as a result of incident radiation of a shorter wavelength.

Fractal.definition: A complex geometric pattern that is self-similar across different scales.

Fusion.definition: A nuclear reaction in which atomic nuclei of low atomic number fuse to form a heavier nucleus with the release of energy.

Gamete.definition: A mature haploid male or female germ cell which is able to unite with another of the opposite sex in sexual reproduction to form a zygote.

Game Theory.definition: The study of mathematical models of strategic interaction among rational decision-makers.

Gene Expression.definition: The process by which information from a gene is used in the synthesis of a functional gene product.

General Relativity.definition: Einstein's theory of gravitation, where gravity is explained as the curvature of spacetime.

Genetic Drift.definition: Variation in the relative frequency of different genotypes in a small population, owing to the chance disappearance of particular genes.

Genome.definition: The complete set of genes or genetic material present in a cell or organism.

Glassmorphism.definition: A UI design trend characterized by semi-transparent backgrounds with a background blur, resembling frosted glass.

Gluon.definition: A subatomic particle of a class that is thought to bind quarks together.

Graphene.definition: A form of carbon consisting of a single layer of atoms arranged in a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice.

Graviton.definition: A hypothetical quantum of gravitational energy, regarded as a particle.

Hadron.definition: A subatomic composite particle made of quarks held together by the strong force.

Half-life.definition: The time taken for the radioactivity of a specified isotope to fall to half its original value.

Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle.definition: A fundamental theory in quantum mechanics stating that one cannot simultaneously know both the exact position and the exact momentum of a particle.

Higgs Boson.definition: An elementary particle in the Standard Model of particle physics produced by the quantum excitation of the Higgs field, giving mass to other particles.

Homeostasis.definition: The tendency toward a relatively stable equilibrium between interdependent elements, especially as maintained by physiological processes.

Host-Associated Differentiation.definition: The process where populations of a parasite or herbivore evolve differences based on the host species they utilize.

Hubble's Law.definition: The observation in physical cosmology that galaxies are moving away from the Earth at speeds proportional to their distance.

Inertia.definition: The tendency of objects in motion to stay in motion, and objects at rest to stay at rest, unless acted upon by an outside force.

Ionization.definition: The process by which an atom or a molecule acquires a negative or positive charge by gaining or losing electrons.

Isotope.definition: Each of two or more forms of the same element that contain equal numbers of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

Karyotype.definition: The number and visual appearance of the chromosomes in the cell nuclei of an organism or species.

Kinetic Energy.definition: Energy which a body possesses by virtue of being in motion.

Kugelblitz.definition: A theoretical concentration of light so intense that it forms an event horizon and becomes self-trapped, effectively a black hole made of radiation.

Lagrange Point.definition: Points in space where the gravitational forces of a two-body system like the Sun and Earth produce enhanced regions of attraction and repulsion.

Lepton.definition: A subatomic particle, such as an electron, muon, or neutrino, that does not take part in the strong interaction.

LIDAR.definition: A detection system that works on the principle of radar, but uses light from a laser to measure distances.

Lymphocyte.definition: A form of small leukocyte (white blood cell) with a single round nucleus, occurring especially in the lymphatic system.

Machine Learning.definition: A branch of artificial intelligence (AI) and computer science which focuses on the use of data and algorithms to imitate the way that humans learn.

Magnetosphere.definition: The region surrounding a planet in which its magnetic field is the predominant effective magnetic field.

Meiosis.definition: A type of cell division that results in four daughter cells each with half the number of chromosomes of the parent cell.

Metabolism.definition: The chemical processes that occur within a living organism in order to maintain life.

Microbiome.definition: The collection of all microorganisms, such as bacteria, fungi, and viruses, that naturally live on and inside our bodies.

Mitochondria.definition: Organelles in which the biochemical processes of respiration and energy production occur.

Mitosis.definition: A type of cell division that results in two daughter cells each having the same number and kind of chromosomes as the parent nucleus.

Nanotechnology.definition: The branch of technology that deals with dimensions and tolerances of less than 100 nanometers, especially the manipulation of individual atoms and molecules.

Natural Selection.definition: The process whereby organisms better adapted to their environment tend to survive and produce more offspring.

Neural Network.definition: A computer system modeled on the human brain and nervous system.

Neuroplasticity.definition: The ability of the brain to form and reorganize synaptic connections, especially in response to learning or experience.

Neutron Star.definition: An extremely dense star composed almost entirely of neutrons, resulting from the gravitational collapse of a massive star after a supernova.

Obsidian.definition: A hard, dark, glasslike volcanic rock formed by the rapid solidification of lava without crystallization.

Organelle.definition: Any of a number of organized or specialized structures within a living cell.

Osmosis.definition: A process by which molecules of a solvent tend to pass through a semipermeable membrane from a less concentrated solution into a more concentrated one.

Oxidation.definition: A chemical reaction that involves the loss of electrons or an increase in the oxidation state of a molecule, atom, or ion.

Parallax.definition: The apparent displacement or difference in the apparent position of an object viewed along two different lines of sight.

Pathogen.definition: A bacterium, virus, or other microorganism that can cause disease.

Phenotype.definition: The set of observable characteristics of an individual resulting from the interaction of its genotype with the environment.

Photon.definition: A particle representing a quantum of light or other electromagnetic radiation.

Photosynthesis.definition: The process by which green plants and some other organisms use sunlight to synthesize foods with the aid of chlorophyll.

Plasma.definition: An ionized gas consisting of positive ions and free electrons in proportions that result in more or less no overall electric charge.

Polymer.definition: A substance that has a molecular structure consisting chiefly or entirely of a large number of similar units bonded together.

Positron.definition: A subatomic particle with the same mass as an electron and a numerically equal but positive charge.

Prion.definition: An infectious protein particle similar to a virus but lacking nucleic acid.

Quantum Entanglement.definition: A physical phenomenon that occurs when pairs or groups of particles are generated, interact, or share spatial proximity such that the quantum state of each particle cannot be described independently.

Quantum Mechanics.definition: The branch of physics that deals with the behavior of matter and light on the atomic and subatomic scale.

Quark.definition: Any of a number of subatomic particles carrying a fractional electric charge and postulated as building blocks of the hadrons.

Quasar.definition: A massive and extremely remote celestial object, emitting exceptionally large amounts of energy, typically having a starlike image in a telescope.

Radioactivity.definition: The emission of ionizing radiation or particles caused by the spontaneous disintegration of atomic nuclei.

Redshift.definition: The displacement of spectral lines toward longer wavelengths (the red end of the spectrum) in radiation from distant galaxies and celestial objects.

Refraction.definition: The change in direction of a wave passing from one medium to another caused by its change in speed.

Ribosome.definition: A minute particle consisting of RNA and associated proteins found in large numbers in the cytoplasm of living cells.

RNA.definition: Ribonucleic acid, a nucleic acid present in all living cells, acting as a messenger carrying instructions from DNA.

Schrödinger's Cat.definition: A thought experiment that illustrates a paradox of quantum superposition.

Singularity.definition: A point at which a function takes an infinite value, especially in space-time when matter is infinitely dense, as at the center of a black hole.

Spacetime.definition: The four-dimensional continuum of one temporal and three spatial coordinates in which any physical event can be located.

Speciation.definition: The formation of new and distinct species in the course of evolution.

String Theory.definition: A theoretical framework in which the point-like particles of particle physics are replaced by one-dimensional objects called strings.

Superconductivity.definition: The property of zero electrical resistance and expulsion of magnetic flux fields occurring in certain materials when cooled below a characteristic critical temperature.

Symbiosis.definition: Interaction between two different organisms living in close physical association, typically to the advantage of both.

Synapse.definition: A junction between two nerve cells, consisting of a minute gap across which impulses pass by diffusion of a neurotransmitter.

Teleportation.definition: The theoretical transfer of matter or energy from one point to another without traversing the physical space between them.

Telomere.definition: A compound structure at the end of a chromosome.

Thermodynamics.definition: The branch of physical science that deals with the relations between heat and other forms of energy.

Transcription.definition: The process by which the information in a strand of DNA is copied into a new molecule of messenger RNA.

Translation.definition: The process by which a sequence of nucleotide triplets in a messenger RNA molecule gives rise to a specific sequence of amino acids during synthesis of a polypeptide or protein.

Valence Electron.definition: An outer shell electron that is associated with an atom, and that can participate in the formation of a chemical bond.

Virulence.definition: The severity or harmfulness of a disease or poison.

Viscosity.definition: The state of being thick, sticky, and semi-fluid in consistency, due to internal friction.

Wave-Particle Duality.definition: The concept in quantum mechanics that every particle or quantum entity may be described as either a particle or a wave.

Wormhole.definition: A theoretical passage through space-time that could create shortcuts for long journeys across the universe.

Zero-point Energy.definition: The lowest possible energy that a quantum mechanical physical system may have.

Zoonosis.definition: A disease which can be transmitted to humans from animals.

Zygote.definition: A diploid cell resulting from the fusion of two haploid gametes; a fertilized ovum.
